steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Two findings must be combined: (1) Tarabieh found that higher perceived environmental risk reduces purchase likelihood. (2) The Brazil study found college students had the highest environmental risk perception among all demographic groups. Assuming Tarabieh's results apply broadly, the blank must state what follows logically.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'college students likely prioritize other factors over environmental sustainability when purchasing': The logic chain says high risk perception lowers purchase likelihood — not that students ignore sustainability. This contradicts Tarabieh's finding rather than applying it. ✗
  • Choice B — 'college students might be less likely than participants in the other demographic groups to purchase the skin-care product': Highest environmental risk perception + Tarabieh's rule (higher perception = lower purchase likelihood) = college students are the least likely to buy. Logically sound. ✓
  • Choice C — 'there is not a meaningful difference in purchase likelihood among demographic groups': The data shows college students stand out with the highest perception, implying a meaningful difference — the opposite of this choice. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the skin-care product is more appealing to college students than other similar products are': The passage compares demographic groups, not different products. Nothing supports a claim about relative product appeal. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
